lines: The warden kept a private book inside a hollowed hymnal. The early pages are an officer at his work: counts of the collared, hours of litany kept, names struck through when a body stopped opening or stopped breathing.

The middle pages turn. 'The Stilling has held a year. The carts still come up the Hallowfen road, but the people they unload are not sick. No fever. No gold in the eye. They argued a tithe, or asked the wrong thing at a gate. I am to set them to the litany until they are quiet. I have stopped writing down what quiet costs. The ones who go all the way quiet I take down below the cellar and keep in order, sorted the way the diocese sorts everything. There is more room down there than this house has ever admitted to having.'

The last clear entry is worse. 'No orders this month, and no supplies. The diocese has forgotten the house, or wants it forgotten. I have put my pay and my seal in the wall box behind the saint, up where I hung the worst of them as a warning. The key is not on me. It is behind a loose stone, low in the wall of the westmost cell, where we kept the loud ones. If you are reading this, you came in past whatever has moved in below. Take it and go.'
A final line is barely pressed into the paper. 'I prayed too, at the end. It did not help me either.'
